title: 第 55 節
en: 3. ' That Self abides in the heart. And this is the etymological explanation. The heart is called hrzdayam, instead of hrzdy-ayam, i. e. He who is in the heart. He who knows this, that He is in the heart, goes day by day (when in sushupti, deep sleep) into heaven (svarga), i.e. into the Brahman of the heart.

4. ' Now that serene being2 which, after having 1 All the desires mentioned before are fulfilled, if we find their fulfilment in our Self, in the city of Brahman within our heart. There we always can possess those whom we have loved, only we must not wish to see them with our eyes; that would be a false covering to a true desire.
2 Cf. JO. Up. VIII, 12, 3.
[3] K I 30 A'ff ANDOGYA-UPANISHAD.

risen from out this earthly body, and having reached the highest light (self-knowledge), appears in its true form, that is the Self,' thus he spoke (when asked by his pupils). This is the immortal, the fearless, this is Brahman. And of that Brahman the name is the True, Satyam, 5. This name Sattyam consists of three syllables, sat-ti-yam *. Sat signifies the immortal, t, the mortal, and with yam he binds both. Because he binds both, the immortal and the mortal, therefore it is yam. He who knows this goes day by day into heaven (svarga).
Fourth Kha.zv.da.

1. That Self is a bank2, a boundary, so that these worlds may not be confounded. Day and night do not pass that bank, nor old age, death, and grief; neither good nor evil deeds. All evil-doers turn back from it, for the world of Brahman is free from all evil.

2. Therefore he who has crossed that bank, if blind, ceases to be blind ; if wounded, ceases to be wounded ; if afflicted, ceases to be afflicted. Therefore when that bank has been crossed, night becomes day indeed, for the world of Brahman is lighted up once for all 3.

3. And that world of Brahman belongs to those 1 We ought probably to read Sattyam, and then Sat-ti-yam. The 1 in ti would then be the dual of an anubandha 1. Instead of yaddhi, I conjecture yatti. See Ait. Arawyaka II, 5, 5.

2 Setu, generally translated by bridge, was originally a bank of earth (mr/dadimaya), thrown up to serve as a pathway (pons) through water or a swamp. Such banks exist still in many places, and they serve at the same time as boundaries (maryada) between fields belonging to different properties. Cf. Mait. Up. VII, 7 ; Ka//z. Up. Ill, 2 ; Talav. Up. comm. p. 59; Mu»</. Up. II, 2, 5.
3 Kh. Up. Ill, 11, 3.
VIII PRAPA7WAKA, 5 KHANDA, 3. I 31 only who find it by abstinence — for them there is freedom in all the worlds.
Fifth Khanda.
1. What people call sacrifice (ya^iia), that is really abstinence (brahma/warya). For he who knows, obtains that (world of Brahman, which others obtain by sacrifice), by means of abstinence.
What people call sacrifice (ish/a), that is really abstinence, for by abstinence, having searched (ishAra), he obtains the Self.
2. What people call sacrifice (sattraya^a), that is really abstinence, for by abstinence he obtains from the Sat (the true), the safety (tra^a) of the Self.
What people call the vow of silence (mauna), that is really abstinence, for he who by abstinence has found out the Self, meditates (manute).
3. What people call fasting (anas'akayana), that is really abstinence, for that Self does not perish (na naiyati), which we find out by abstinence.

What people call a hermit's life (ara^yayana), that is really abstinence. Ara x and YVya are two lakes in the world of Brahman, in the third heaven from hence ; and there is the lake Airammadiya, and the Aivattha tree, showering down Soma, and the city of Brahman (Hira^yagarbha) Apara^ita 2, and the golden Prabhuvimita (the hall built by Prabhu, Brahman).
Now that world of Brahman belongs to those who find the lakes Ara and iVya in the world of Brahman by means of abstinence ; for them there is freedom in all the worlds3.
1 In the Kaush. Br. Up. I, 3, the lake is called Ara, at least according to the commentator.
2 In the Kaush. Br. Up. Apara^-ita is not pM, but ayatanam.
3 The fifth khzfida. is chiefly meant to recommend brahma^arya K 2 132 ATtfANDOGYA-UPANISHAD.
Sixth Khanda.

1. Now those arteries of the heart consist of a brown substance, of a white, blue, yellow, and red or abstinence from all worldly enjoyments, enjoined on the brahma&irin, the student, as a means of obtaining a knowledge of Brahman. But instead of showing that such abstinence is indispensable for a proper concentration of our intellectual faculties, we are told that abstinence is the same as certain sacrifices ; and this is shown, not by arguments, but by a number of very far-fetched plays on words. These it is impossible to render in any translation, nay, they hardly deserve being translated. Thus abstinence is said to be identical with sacrifice, yagna,, because yo g"fiata, 'he who knows,' has a certain similarity with ya^iia. Ish/a, another kind of sacrifice, is compared with eshazza, search; sattrayawa with Sat, the True, the Brahman, and traya/za, protection; raauna, silence, with manana, meditating (which may be right); ana.yakayana, fasting, with naj, to perish, and ara/zyagana, a hermit's life, with ara, nya, and ayana, going to the two lakes Ara and iVya, which are believed to exist in the legendary world of Brahman. Nothing can be more absurd. Having once struck the note of Brahmanic legends, such as we find it, for instance, in the Kaushitaki-brahmazza-upanishad, the author goes on. Besides the lakes Ara and iVya (in the Kaushitaki-brahmazza-upanishad we have only one lake, called Ara), he mentions the Airammadiya lake, and explains it as aira (ira annam, tanmaya airo mazz</as, tena purzzam airam) and madiya, delightful. The A^vattha tree, which pours down Soma, is not tortured into anything else, except that Soma is explained as the immortal, or nectar. Apara^-ita becomes the city of Brahman, because it can be conquered by no one except those who have practised abstinence. And the hall which elsewhere is called Vibhu-pramita becomes Prabhuvimitam, or Prabhu-vinirmita, madebyPrabhu, i.e. Brahman. All the fulfilled desires, as enumerated in khazz</as 2-5, whether the finding again of our fathers and mothers, or entering the Brahmaloka with its lakes and palaces, must be taken, not as material (sthula), but as mental only (manasa). On that account, however, they are by no means considered as false or unreal, as little as dreams are. Dreams are false and unreal, relatively only, i. e. relatively to what we see, when we awake ; but not in themselves. Whatever we see in waking, also, has been shown to be VIII PRAPAFtfAKA, 6 KHANDA, 5. I 33 substance, and so is the sun brown, white, blue, yellow, and red.

2. As a very long highway goes to two places, to one at the beginning, and to another at the end, so do the rays of the sun go to both worlds, to this one and to the other. They start from the sun, and enter into those arteries ; they start from those arteries, and enter into the sun.
3. And when a man is asleep, reposing, and at perfect rest, so that he sees no dream \ then he has entered into those arteries. Then no evil touches him, for he has obtained the light (of the sun).
4. And when a man falls ill, then those who sit round him, say, ' Do you know me ? Do you know me?' As long as he has not departed from this body, he knows them.

5. But when he departs from this body, then he departs upwards by those very rays (towards the worlds which he has gained by merit, not by knowledge) ; or he goes out while meditating on Om (and thus securing an entrance into the Brahmafalse; because it consists of forms and names only; yet these forms and names have a true element in them, viz. the Sat. Before we know that Sat, all the objects we see in waking seem true ; as dreams seem true in dreaming. But when once we awake from our waking by true knowledge, we see that nothing is true but the Sat. When we imagine we see a serpent, and then discover that it is a rope, the serpent disappears as false, but what was true in it, the rope, remains true.

1 Svapna in Sanskrit is both somnus and s omnium. Hence one might translate also, ' so that he is not aware that he is asleep,' which in some respects would seem even more appropriate in our passage; cf. VIII, 11, 1.
zh: 3. 「自我（Self）居住於心中。這是字源學的解釋。心臟被稱為 hrzdayam，而非 hrzdy-ayam，亦即「居住於心中者」。凡知曉此理——祂居住於心中——者，每日（當處於 sushupti，深度睡眠時）進入天界（svarga），即進入心中的梵（Brahman）。
4. 「現在那個安詳的存在2，在我們所有之前提到的慾望都得以滿足之後，如果我們在自我之中、在我們心中梵天城（Brahman）裡發現它們的滿足，就會出現。在那裡，我們始終能夠擁有我們所愛的人，只是我們不應該希望用眼睛看到他們；那將是對真實慾望的虛假遮蔽。
2 參見《耆那教奧義書》第八卷第十二章第三節。
[3] K I 30 以下 《歌者奧義書》。

升起自此塵世之身，抵達最高之光（自我認識），以其真實之形顯現，即為自我。』如是他言（當弟子們問及時）。此為不朽者，無畏者，此為梵（Brahman）。而梵之名為真實，薩特亞（Satyam），5。此名薩特亞（Sattyam）由三音節組成，薩-蒂-亞（sat-ti-yam）*。薩（sat）表示不朽者，蒂（t）表示有朽者，而以亞（yam）束縛二者。因其束縛不朽者與有朽者二者，故稱為亞（yam）。知此者日日昇入天界（svarga）。
第四章 語言的疾病
那個自我是一道堤壩、一條界限，以便這些世界不致相混。日與夜不能越過那道堤壩，老年、死亡和悲痛也不能，善惡業行同樣不能。一切作惡者都在它面前退縮，因為梵的世界乃是完全遠離一切邪惡的。
2. 因此，已經渡過彼岸的人，如果是盲者，就不再是盲者；如果是傷者，就不再是傷者；如果是病苦者，就不再是病苦者。因此當那彼岸已被渡過時，夜確實變成了白晝，因為梵的世界一旦被照亮，便永遠被照亮3。
3. 那婆羅門的世界屬於那些¹ 我們大概應該讀作 Sattyam，其後為 Sat-ti-yam。其中 ti 裡的 1 便是某個尾注標記（anubandha）的雙數形式。我推測 yaddhi 應讀作 yatti。參見《愛達羅耶卡奧義書》第二卷，第五篇，第五節。
2 Setu 通常譯為橋梁，原本是指一堆土堤（mr/dadimaya），用作穿過水域或沼澤地帶的通路（pons）。這類土堤至今仍存在於許多地方，同時也可用作界線（maryada），以分隔屬於不同地產的田野。參見 Mait. Up. VII, 7；Ka//z. Up. III, 2；Talav. Up. comm. p. 59；Mu». Up. II, 2, 5。
3 Kh. Up. III, 11, 3.
VIII 普拉帕塔卡（Prapathaka），第五甘達（Khanda），第三章。「惟有藉由禁慾而得悟者——對他們而言，在一切世界中都有自由。」
第五分篇
人們所謂的祭祀（yajña），實際上是禁慾（brahmacarya）。蓋凡知曉此理者，乃藉禁慾之力而獲得彼（梵天世界，乃他人藉祭祀所得者）。
人們所謂的祭祀（ish/a），實際上是禁慾，因為透過禁慾，經過探求（ishAra），他獲得了真我（Self）。
人們所謂的祭祀（sattraya^a），實則是禁慾，因為透過禁慾，他從薩特（Sat，真理）獲得自我的安全（tra^a）。
人們所謂的沈默誓願（mauna），其實是苦行，因為通過苦行而發現自我的人，他就會冥想（manute）。
3. 人們所謂的禁食（anas'akayana），那實在是一種節制，因為那個自我（Self）並不會消亡（na naiyati），而我們正是藉由節制來發現這一點。

人們所謂的隱士生活（arayayana），那實在就是禁慾。阿拉克（Ara x）與毘耶（YVya）是梵天世界中的兩座湖泊，位於此處上方第三重天；此外還有艾拉瑪迪耶湖（the lake Airammadiya）、艾瓦塔樹（the Aivattha tree），降灑索摩甘露，以及梵天城（Hiraṇyagarbha）阿波羅那（Aparaita），以及黃金色的普拉布維米塔廳（the golden Prabhuvimita），乃普拉布（毘盧遮那天）所建。
現在那梵天的世界屬於那些通過禁慾在梵天的世界中發現阿羅（Ara）和毗耶（Vya）兩個湖泊的人；對於他們，在所有的世界中都有自由。
根據註釋家的說法，在《考希塔基梵書奧義書》(Kaush. Br. Up.) I, 3 中，湖泊被稱為阿羅（Ara）。
2 在《考什‧梵書‧奧義書》（Kaush. Br. Up.）中，Apara^-ita 不是 pM，而是 ayatanam。
第五卷主要是在推薦梵行（brahma­carya）。
第六分篇

現在這些心臟的脈管由棕色、白色、藍色、黃色和紅色的物質組成，或者說，由對一切世俗享樂的禁欲所組成——這種禁欲乃是對梵行學生（brahmaćirin）所要求的，作為獲得梵天（Brahman）知識的手段。然而，與其說明這種禁欲對於我們智識官能的適當專注是不可或缺的，我們反而被告知禁欲與某些祭祀相同；這並非通過論證來證明，而是通過大量非常牽強的文字遊戲來說明的。這些遊戲在任何翻譯中都無法呈現，事實上，它們幾乎不值得被翻譯。例如，禁欲據稱與祭祀（yajña）相同，因為「認識者」（yajñavid）與 yajña 有某種相似性。另一種祭祀伊施塔（Ishta）被比作「探求」（eshana）；薩特亞雅吉那（sattrayajna）與薩特（Sat，真實、梵天）以及「保護」（traya）相關；靜默（mauna）與「冥想」（manana）相關（這可能是對的）；禁食（anayana）與「滅亡」（naya）相關；隱者生活（araṇyayana）則與阿拉（ara）、尼亞（nya）和「前往」（ayana）相關，據說指的是在梵天的傳說世界中存在的兩個湖泊阿拉湖和尼亞湖。沒有什麼比這更荒謬的了。一旦作者採用了婆羅門傳說的基調——比如在《高希塔基婆羅門奧義書》（Kaushitaki-brahmana-upanishad）中所見的那樣——他就繼續下去。除了阿拉湖和尼亞湖（在《高希塔基婆羅門奧義書》中只有一個名叫阿拉的湖），他提到了艾拉摩迪亞湖（Airammadiya），並將其解釋為「艾拉」（aira）和「迷人的」（madiya）。傾瀉蘇摩（Soma）的阿什瓦塔樹（Aśvattha tree）並未被牽強地解釋成其他東西，只是蘇摩被解釋為「不朽的」或「甘露」。阿帕拉吉塔（Aparajita）變成了梵天之城，因為除了那些修行禁欲的人，沒有人能征服它。而在別處被稱為「毗布普拉密塔」（Vibhu-pramita）的大廳變成了「普拉布維米塔」（Prabhuvimitam）或「普拉布維尼米塔」（Prabhu-vinirmita），即「由普拉布創造」，亦即梵天。在《奧義書》第二至五頌中列舉的所有滿足的欲望——無論是再次找到我們的父親母親，還是進入具有湖泊和宮殿的梵天界——都不應被視為物質的（sthula），而只應被視為心理的（manasa）。然而，正因為如此，它們絕非被認為是虛假或非真實的，正如夢境也絕非虛假或非真實的一樣。夢境是虛假和非真實的，只是相對而言——相對於我們醒時所見的事物而言；但在其自身並非如此。無論我們在醒時所見到的任何東西，也都已被證明為是實體；因此太陽是棕色、白色、藍色、黃色和紅色的。
2. 如同一條漫長的道路通往兩處，始於一端，終於另一端，太陽的光線亦然，照耀於兩個世界——此世與彼世。光線自太陽出發，進入那些脈道；又自那些脈道出發，進入太陽。
3. 當一個人睡眠、安憩、完全靜止，以致於他看不到夢境時，他便進入了這些脈絡。此時沒有邪惡觸及他，因為他已經獲得了（太陽的）光明。
4. 當一個人患病時，坐在他身邊的人會說：「你認識我嗎？你認識我嗎？」只要他還沒有離開這個身體，他就認識他們。

5. 然而當他離棄此身時，他便沿著那些光線向上而行（朝向他因業行而非知識所獲得的世界）；或者他在冥想唵（Om）時出離（如此確保進入梵天境界）。但那梵天是虛假的，因為它僅由形相與名稱構成；然而這些形相與名稱之中卻有一個真實的要素存在，即存在者（Sat）。在我們認識存在者之前，所有我們在清醒狀態中所見的事物似乎都是真實的；如同在夢中所見的事物似乎是真實一樣。但一旦我們以真正的知識從清醒狀態中覺醒，我們便看到唯有存在者是真實的，其他都非真實。當我們想像自己看到一條蛇，然後發現那其實是一條繩子時，蛇作為虛假的東西便消失了，但其中真實的要素——繩子——仍然保持真實。
梵文 svapna 既表示「睡眠」，亦表示「夢」。因此人們也可以譯為「以至於他不知曉自己在睡眠」，在某些方面似乎對我們這處經文更為恰當；參閱第八卷第十一章第一節。
